title: Reclaiming Air-Dry Clay from Dry Scraps

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of reconstituting dry or overly stiff clay scraps back to a workable consistency by reintroducing water.
A kneading technique used to homogenise clay and remove air pockets before shaping, producing an even and consistent body.

content.text: Air-dry clay scraps that dried before sealing can be brought back to a workable state.

is not possible once a piece has been sealed with varnish or paint, and it does not work with polymer clay or self-hardening clays that use a chemical rather than water-based drying process. For plain, unsealed air-dry clay, however, it is straightforward and worth doing rather than throwing scraps away.

materials.name: Water
materials.quantity: enough to cover scraps in the container

tools.name: Old cloth or canvas board for drying
Pottery wire cutter or length of wire

content.text: Collect only unsealed, unpainted scraps. Check each piece: if it has a varnished or painted surface, set it aside as it cannot be reclaimed. Break large dry pieces into roughly walnut-sized chunks so water can reach all parts quickly.

Place the broken dry scraps in a bucket or deep container. Pour cold water over them until they are completely submerged with about 25 mm of water above the highest piece. Do not use hot water: it can cause some clays to lose cohesion and become sticky rather than plastic.

Leave the scraps to soak for a minimum of 12 hours, preferably overnight. By the end of soaking the pieces should have softened to a slurry or semi-liquid state. If any large lumps remain hard in the centre, break them up with your hands and leave for another 4-6 hours.

Pour off the standing water from the top of the container. The goal is to remove excess water without pouring away clay-rich liquid at the bottom. Tilt the container slowly and let the water run off from the surface while the clay body settles.

Spread the wet clay on a cloth or canvas board in a layer about 20-25 mm thick. Leave in a room-temperature space with moderate airflow. Check it every few hours, turning the clay mass over with your hands when the top surface begins to stiffen. You are aiming for the clay to reach a soft but plastic consistency, not fully dry again.
When the clay holds its shape without slumping and no longer sticks heavily to your hands, gather it into a ball and begin
. Push the clay away from you with the heel of your hand, fold it back, rotate a quarter turn, and repeat. Continue for 3-5 minutes until the texture is even and consistent throughout.

If the clay feels too soft after wedging, wrap it in a cloth and leave for another hour or two to stiffen slightly before use. If it seems too stiff, dampen your hands and work a small amount of water in during wedging. Store reclaimed clay in an airtight bag with a small damp cloth to keep it usable.

The reclaimed clay feels smooth, plastic, and similar in consistency to fresh clay from a new pack. It holds an impressed fingerprint clearly, does not crack when bent, and pulls away from surfaces cleanly. It behaves the same as fresh clay in shaping.
Trying to reclaim sealed clay: varnish or paint forms a barrier that water cannot penetrate. The surface may appear to soften but the clay beneath stays dry and the result is unusable.
Draining too aggressively and losing clay down the drain with the water. Drain slowly and only from the top of the container where the water is clearest.
Using the clay before the excess moisture has evaporated off: clay that is too wet tears and collapses. Give it enough time on the cloth to reach a workable consistency before wedging.

items.problem: Reclaimed clay is grainy or lumpy after wedging
items.cause: Some pieces were not fully broken down during soaking
items.fix: Soak the batch again with fresh water for another 12 hours, then drain and dry on the cloth as before.

items.problem: Clay is sticky and unworkable even after drying on the cloth
items.cause: Too much water remaining in the clay body
items.fix: Spread the clay more thinly on the cloth and allow more airflow. Turning it every hour will speed up even moisture loss.

items.problem: Clay feels weak or crumbly once shaped and dried
items.cause: Repeated reclaiming degrades the binders in some air-dry clay formulations
items.fix: Mix the reclaimed clay with an equal portion of fresh clay from the same brand to restore strength.
